verdict: Moderate displacement risk, with potential for significant job disruption in the next decade.

reasoning_steps.title: Routine Cognitive Tasks
reasoning_steps.body: Tasks 1, 2, and 7 involve creating or maintaining visual documentation, recommending process changes, and developing specifications for wind technology components. These tasks are increasingly automated by LLMs.

reasoning_steps.title: Creative Judgment
reasoning_steps.body: Tasks 4 and 11 require investigating experimental technologies and monitoring wind farm construction to ensure compliance with regulatory standards. While some creative judgment is still required, these tasks are becoming more routine.

reasoning_steps.title: Regulatory Moat
reasoning_steps.body: The regulatory moat for this occupation is relatively weak due to the lack of licensure requirements and the presence of many consulting firms that provide similar services.

reasoning_steps.title: Embodied Work
reasoning_steps.body: Tasks 8, 9, and 10 involve testing wind turbine components, overseeing subcontractor work, and monitoring construction. These tasks require physical presence on-site, which is becoming less common with AI-powered remote monitoring.

reasoning_steps.title: Stakes of Error
reasoning_steps.body: The stakes of error for this occupation are moderate due to the potential consequences of wind turbine failures or non-compliance with regulatory standards.

reasoning_steps.title: Relationships
reasoning_steps.body: Tasks 5 and 12 involve collaborating with designers, consultants, and other stakeholders. While some relationship-building is still required, these tasks are becoming more routine with AI-powered communication tools.

reasoning_steps.title: AI Capability Gap
reasoning_steps.body: The AI capability gap for this occupation is relatively small due to the presence of many consulting firms that provide similar services and the increasing availability of AI-powered remote monitoring tools.

reasoning_steps.title: Physical Dexterity
reasoning_steps.body: Tasks 8, 9, and 10 require physical dexterity and manual labor, which are becoming less common with AI-powered automation.

evidence_ledger.meta: Authority on wind energy engineering tasks

adjacent_careers.name: Wind Turbine Technician
adjacent_careers.reasoning: Both roles require physical presence on-site and manual labor, but wind turbine technicians focus more on maintenance and repair.

adjacent_careers.name: Electrical Engineer
adjacent_careers.reasoning: Both roles involve designing and developing electrical systems for wind farms, but electrical engineers focus more on the technical aspects.

recent_developments.headline: AI coding assistants see broad enterprise adoption
recent_developments.impact: Major firms report 25–30% of new code is now AI-generated and reviewed by engineers.

recent_developments.headline: Labor market data shows AI exposure rising for cognitive jobs
recent_developments.impact: Goldman Sachs and OECD analyses converge on roughly two-thirds of jobs being partially exposed.

recent_developments.headline: Education and tooling shift to assume AI is in the loop
recent_developments.impact: Universities and bootcamps update curricula to emphasize judgment and AI direction over rote production.

quotes.quote: Software engineering will be very different by the end of 2025. Most code will be written by AI, with human engineers reviewing and directing.
quotes.speaker: Dario Amodei
quotes.title: CEO, Anthropic

quotes.quote: AI is going to be the biggest technological shift of our lifetimes. Bigger than mobile, bigger than the web. Inside Microsoft, more than 30 percent of the code in some projects is now written by AI.
quotes.speaker: Satya Nadella
quotes.title: CEO, Microsoft

quotes.quote: More than a quarter of new code at Google is generated by AI, then reviewed and accepted by engineers.
quotes.speaker: Sundar Pichai
quotes.title: CEO, Google / Alphabet
